<ɵ>component</ɵ>slink","matchLevel":"full"}}}},{"url":"https://angular.dev/guide/components/styling#viewencapsulationemulated","content":"By default, Angular uses emulated encapsulation so that a component's styles only apply to elements
defined in that component's template. In this mode, the framework generates a unique HTML attribute
for each component instance, adds that attribute to elements in the component's template, and
inserts that attribute into the CSS selectors defined in your component's styles.
This mode ensures that a component's styles do not leak out and affect other components. However,
global styles defined outside of a component may still affect elements inside a component with
emulated encapsulation.
In emulated mode, Angular supports
the :host pseudo-class.
While the :host-context() pseudo-class
is deprecated in modern browsers, Angular's compiler provides full support for it. Both pseudo-classes
can be used without relying on native
Shadow DOM.
During compilation, the framework transforms these pseudo classes into attributes so it doesn't
comply with these native pseudo classes' rules at runtime (e.g. browser compatibility, specificity). Angular's
emulated encapsulation mode does not support any other pseudo classes related to Shadow DOM, such
as ::shadow or ::part.","type":"content","hierarchy":{"lvl0":"Docs","lvl1":"Styling components","lvl2":"Style scopinglink","lvl3":"ViewEncapsulation.Emulatedlink","lvl4":null,"lvl5":null,"lvl6":null},"objectID":"8-https://angular.dev/guide/components/styling","_snippetResult":{"content":{"value":"By default, Angular uses emulated encapsulation so that a <ɵ>component</ɵ> …","matchLevel":"full"},"hierarchy":{"lvl1":{"value":"Styling <ɵ>component</ɵ>s","matchLevel":"full"},"lvl2":{"value":"Style scopinglink","matchLevel":"none"},"lvl3":{"value":"ViewEncapsulation.Emulatedlink","matchLevel":"none"}}}},{"url":"https://angular.dev/guide/components/selectors#the-not-pseudo-class","content":"Angular supports the :not pseudo-class.
You can append this pseudo-class to any other selector to narrow which elements a component's
selector matches. For example, you could define a [dropzone] attribute selector and prevent
matching textarea elements:
Angular does not support any other pseudo-classes or pseudo-elements in component selectors.","type":"content","hierarchy":{"lvl0":"Docs","lvl1":"Component selectors","lvl2":"Types of selectorslink","lvl3":"The :not pseudo-classlink","lvl4":null,"lvl5":null,"lvl6":null},"objectID":"8-https://angular.dev/guide/components/selectors","_snippetResult":{"content":{"value":"… to narrow which elements a <ɵ>component</ɵ>'s
selector matches. For …","matchLevel":"full"},"hierarchy":{"lvl1":{"value":"<ɵ>Component</ɵ> selectors","matchLevel":"full"},"lvl2":{"value":"Types of selectorslink","matchLevel":"none"},"lvl3":{"value":"The :not pseudo-classlink","matchLevel":"none"}}}},{"url":"https://angular.dev/guide/components/queries#content-queries","content":"Content queries retrieve results from the elements in the component's content— the elements nested inside the component in the template where it's used. You can query for a single result with the contentChild function.
If the query does not find a result, its value is undefined. This may occur if the target element is absent or hidden by @if. Angular keeps the result of contentChild up to date as your application state changes.
By default, content queries find only direct children of the component and do not traverse into descendants.
You can also query for multiple results with the contentChildren function.
contentChildren creates a signal with an Array of the query results.
Queries never pierce through component boundaries. <ɵ>component</ɵ>slink","matchLevel":"full"}}}},{"url":"https://angular.dev/guide/components/styling#viewencapsulationemulated","content":"By default, Angular uses emulated encapsulation so that a component's styles only apply to elements
defined in that component's template. In this mode, the framework generates a unique HTML attribute
for each component instance, adds that attribute to elements in the component's template, and
inserts that attribute into the CSS selectors defined in your component's styles.
This mode ensures that a component's styles do not leak out and affect other components. However,
global styles defined outside of a component may still affect elements inside a component with
emulated encapsulation.
In emulated mode, Angular supports
the :host pseudo-class.
While the :host-context() pseudo-class
is deprecated in modern browsers, Angular's compiler provides full support for it. Both pseudo-classes
can be used without relying on native
Shadow DOM.
During compilation, the framework transforms these pseudo classes into attributes so it doesn't
comply with these native pseudo classes' rules at runtime (e.g. browser compatibility, specificity). Angular's
emulated encapsulation mode does not support any other pseudo classes related to Shadow DOM, such
as ::shadow or ::part.","type":"content","hierarchy":{"lvl0":"Docs","lvl1":"Styling components","lvl2":"Style scopinglink","lvl3":"ViewEncapsulation.Emulatedlink","lvl4":null,"lvl5":null,"lvl6":null},"objectID":"8-https://angular.dev/guide/components/styling","_snippetResult":{"content":{"value":"By default, Angular uses emulated encapsulation so that a <ɵ>component</ɵ> …","matchLevel":"full"},"hierarchy":{"lvl1":{"value":"Styling <ɵ>component</ɵ>s","matchLevel":"full"},"lvl2":{"value":"Style scopinglink","matchLevel":"none"},"lvl3":{"value":"ViewEncapsulation.Emulatedlink","matchLevel":"none"}}}},{"url":"https://angular.dev/guide/components/selectors#the-not-pseudo-class","content":"Angular supports the :not pseudo-class.
You can append this pseudo-class to any other selector to narrow which elements a component's
selector matches. For example, you could define a [dropzone] attribute selector and prevent
matching textarea elements:
Angular does not support any other pseudo-classes or pseudo-elements in component selectors.","type":"content","hierarchy":{"lvl0":"Docs","lvl1":"Component selectors","lvl2":"Types of selectorslink","lvl3":"The :not pseudo-classlink","lvl4":null,"lvl5":null,"lvl6":null},"objectID":"8-https://angular.dev/guide/components/selectors","_snippetResult":{"content":{"value":"… to narrow which elements a <ɵ>component</ɵ>'s
selector matches. For …","matchLevel":"full"},"hierarchy":{"lvl1":{"value":"<ɵ>Component</ɵ> selectors","matchLevel":"full"},"lvl2":{"value":"Types of selectorslink","matchLevel":"none"},"lvl3":{"value":"The :not pseudo-classlink","matchLevel":"none"}}}},{"url":"https://angular.dev/guide/components/queries#content-queries","content":"Content queries retrieve results from the elements in the component's content— the elements nested inside the component in the template where it's used. You can query for a single result with the contentChild function.
If the query does not find a result, its value is undefined. This may occur if the target element is absent or hidden by @if. Angular keeps the result of contentChild up to date as your application state changes.
By default, content queries find only direct children of the component and do not traverse into descendants.
You can also query for multiple results with the contentChildren function.
contentChildren creates a signal with an Array of the query results.
Queries never pierce through component boundaries. Content queries can only retrieve results from the same template as the component itself.","type":"content","hierarchy":{"lvl0":"Docs","lvl1":"Referencing component children with queries","lvl2":"Content querieslink","lvl3":null,"lvl4":null,"lvl5":null,"lvl6":null},"objectID":"8-https://angular.dev/guide/components/queries","_snippetResult":{"content":{"value":"Content queries retrieve results from the elements in the <ɵ>component</ɵ> …","matchLevel":"full"},"hierarchy":{"lvl1":{"value":"Referencing <ɵ>component</ɵ> children with queries","matchLevel":"full"},"lvl2":{"value":"Content querieslink","matchLevel":"none"}}}},{"url":"https://angular.dev/guide/components/programmatic-rendering#passing-inputs-to-dynamically-rendered-components","content":"You can pass inputs to the dynamically rendered component using the ngComponentOutletInputs property. This property accepts an object where keys are input names and values are the input values.
